title: Superconductivity in the pressure-amorphized topological insulator CrP$_4$
authors: Chutong Zhang, Xiangzhuo Xing, Na Zuo, Bowen Zheng, Bin Li, Jiajia Feng, Xiaolei Yi, Yan Meng, Xiaoran Zhang, Bingchao Yang, Chao Wang, Xin Chen, Yongsheng Zhang, Xiaofeng Xu, Xiaobing Liu
abstract: The interplay among superconductivity, magnetism, and nontrivial band topology represents one of the most compelling frontiers in condensed matter physics. The exploration of novel superconductivity in 3d transition-metal compounds, particularly the rare Cr-based systems containing strongly magnetic Cr ions, has long attracted attention owing to their unconventional pairing mechanisms that challenge conventional wisdom. Yet, Cr-based superconductors remain scarce, especially those possessing nontrivial topological character, underscoring the urgent need to uncover new members. Here we report the observation of superconductivity in pressure-amorphized Cr-based topological insulator CrP$_4$. Upon compression, CrP$_4$ undergoes an anomalous quantum phase transition from a metallic to a semiconducting-like state at around 15 GPa, driven by significant changes in the electronic structure. At approximately 70 GPa, re-metallization with superconductivity occurs alongside an irreversible amorphization. The superconducting transition temperature Tc increases monotonically with pressure, reaching 4.8 K at 141.3 GPa. Furthermore, theoretical calculations predict multiple topological phase transitions from a strong topological insulator to a trivial state and finally back to a strong topological state under pressure. Our study not only establishes CrP$_4$ as the first Cr-based amorphous superconductor but also opens a new paradigm for exploring superconducting and topological properties in amorphous materials.
summary: This work reports the discovery of superconductivity in pressure-amorphized $	ext{CrP}_4$, linking it to a complex sequence of electronic and topological phase transitions. By combining high-pressure experiments with DFT calculations, the authors establish that structural disorder can induce unconventional superconducting states in topologically rich materials.
main_problem: The study investigates the complex interplay between superconductivity, magnetism, and nontrivial band topology in chromium-based materials under extreme pressure.
main_result: Superconductivity was observed in the pressure-amorphized $	ext{CrP}_4$, establishing it as a first example of a Cr-based amorphous superconductor. The material exhibits multiple quantum phase transitions, including topological changes and structural amorphization, all correlated with superconductivity onset.
method: The research combines high-pressure experimental measurements (resistivity, Hall effect, XRD) with advanced theoretical calculations using DFT to map out the electronic structure evolution.
model_or_system: The system is $	ext{CrP}_4$, a transition-metal tetraphosphide. The investigation spans both its crystalline phase and an amorphous state induced by high pressure (up to 141.3 GPa). Theoretical modeling incorporates relativistic effects and spin-orbit coupling.
key_observables: Superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$), Hall coefficient sign change, resistance evolution ($ho(P)$), and $\mathbb{Z}_2$ topological invariants.
important_parameters: Pressure (up to 141.3 GPa), Temperature, Magnetic field, Quantum phase transition points ($\sim 15 	ext{ GPa}$ and $\sim 70 	ext{ GPa}$).
main_assumptions: The superconducting pairing mechanism is assumed to be unconventional, likely mediated by spin fluctuations or structural disorder.
figures_summary: Figures summarize the temperature-pressure phase diagram showing multiple transitions (metallic $	o$ semiconducting-like $	o$ metallic) and topological changes in both crystalline and amorphous regimes.
paper_structure: The paper follows a progression from ambient characterization to high-pressure transport measurements, theoretical mapping of topological invariants ($\mathbb{Z}_2$), and finally constructing a comprehensive T-P phase diagram linking structural disorder to superconductivity.
why_interesting: The study provides a model system where extreme external tuning (pressure) drives multiple competing quantum phenomena—topological transitions, magnetic instability, and superconductivity—in an amorphous state, which is highly relevant for understanding emergent quantum states in disordered materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Structural and physical properties of CrP4 at ambient conditions. (a) Crystal structure of CrP4, visualized using VESTA software [53]. (b) Powder XRD pattern obtained with Cu Kα radiation. The solid black line represents the Rietveld refinement. Inset: photograph of a typical CrP4 single crystal. (c)

Figure 1(b) presents the typical XRD pattern, that all the characteristic peaks can be well indexed to a monoclinic structure with the space group C2/c, in good agreement with previous report [28]. Rietveld refinements yields the lattice parameters of a = 5.19165 Å, b= 10.76071 Å, c = 5.77036 Å, and β = 110.6455°. EDX analysis indicates a uniform distribution of Cr and P elements in the produced crystals, with an atomic ratio of ~1:4 (19.91:80.09) (Fig. S2 [42]). Figure 1(c) illustrates the

FIG. 2. Temperature-dependent resistance of CrP4 under pressure. (a) Resistance evolution with pressure up to 75.5 GPa in run 1. (b) and (c) Evolution of resistance with pressure up to 141.3 GPa in run 2. Inset in (c) magnifies the superconducting transition region. (d) and (e) Resistance versus temperature under various magnetic fields at 100.1 GPa and 135.1 GPa in run 2. (f) Upper critical fields μ0Hc2(T) as a function of temperature at different pressures. Tc is defined as the onset of the resistance transition, determined by the intersection of the extrapolated normal-state resistance and the steep transition curve. Solid lines represent fits to the G-L equation.

FIG. 3. Hall effect of CrP4 at 10 K under pressure. (a) and (b) Plots of Hall resistivity ρxy versus magnetic field H under various pressures. (c) Pressure dependence of the Hall coefficient RH, displaying a sign change from positive to negative at the critical pressure of around 15 GPa. (d) The resistance at 10 K and 300 K as a function of pressure for run 2 and run 3. The horizontal dashed lines in (a)-(c) serve as guides to the eye, representing the zero baseline.

FIG. 4. In-situ high-pressure XRD of CrP4. (a) XRD patterns at various pressures up to 146 GPa in run 6. Topmost pattern, labelled as 0(D), taken after pressure release to ambient conditions. (b) Representative Rietveld refinement results for XRD patterns at 2 GPa (up) and 50 GPa (bottom), respectively. (c) Refined lattice parameters (a, b, and c) as a function of pressure. (d) Pressure dependence of the derived cell volume V. Solid red line represents the fit of the experimental data to the third-order B-M equation of state.
